texts.full_text: ip in England and Wales, 1851-1911 by Carry van Lieshout The conventional image of an entrepreneur in Victorian Britain is a captain of industry, heading an engineering or steel factory employing hundreds of workers, and generally pictured with an impressive moustache. But men like that were only the tip of a very large, and much more diverse entrepreneurial iceberg. My research shows that close to 30% of businesses in Victorian Britain were run by women, a proportion that was much larger than hitherto estimated. This work is based on the new British Business Census of Entrepreneurs, created at the University of Cambridge as part of the project ‘Drivers of Entrepreneurship’ under Professor Robert Bennett. The main goal of this study was to characterize isolates of Acanthamoeba obtained in Brazil and evaluate properties associated with their pathogenicity. A total of 39 isolates obtained from keratitis cases (n = 16) and environmental sources (n = 23) were classified into morphological groups and genotyped by sequencing the 18S rDNA fragments ASA.S1 and GTSA.B1. Samples were also tested regarding their thermo-tolerance, osmo-tolerance, and cytopathogenicity in MDCK cells. texts.full_text: 1871) was the second President of the East Alabama College, now known as Auburn University, from 1868 to 1870, and a U.S. Representative from Alabama. Biography James Ferguson Dowdell was born on November 26, 1818, near Monticello, Georgia. Dowdell completed preparatory studies and in 1840 and graduated from Randolph-Macon College, Ashland, Virginia. He studied law. He was admitted to the bar in 1841 and commenced practice in Greenville, Georgia. He moved to Oak Bowery, Alabama, in 1846 and engaged in agricultural pursuits. He was an unsuccessful candidate for election to the State house of representatives in 1849 and 1851. James Dowdell was elected as a Democrat to the Thirty-third, Thirty-fourth, and Thirty-fifth Congreses (March 4, 1863 – March 20, 187). He served on Committee of Expenditures in State Department; District Columbia Appropriations Commission from December 9. Dowdell was appointed as President pro tempore during the absence Dowdell resigned due to health issues and resumed agricultural pursuits at Auburn until his death there September 5 or 6th (reports vary) ,-1870, aged fifty years). He is interred in Pine Hill Cemetery/Auburn Memorial Park.[2] The James F. Downtown Law school Building on the campus of Samford University was named after Dowdell who attended Randolph–Macon College with Judge William P. Sampson for whom it housed until 963 when a fire destroyed law school building which killed two and injuring three others while saving hundreds as many were in class, next door to downtown library.[4] The James F Downtown Law School Building on Samford University campus was named after Dowdell who attended Randolph–Macon College with Judge William P Sampson for whom it housed until 1963 when a fire destroyed law school building which killed two and injuring three others while saving hundreds as many were in class, next door to downtown library. texts.full_text: áu aú distinction apart from etymology? AFAIK there is no internal evidence for any actual phonemic distinction between these diphthongs in Gothic. The distinctions are largely based on etymological grounds, reflecting the Proto-Germanic (and sometimes earlier) forms from which they derive. For instance, the diphthongs and in Gothic often correspond to similar sequences in other Germanic languages and can be traced back to Proto-Germanic au. However, it's important to note that while etymology provides a strong basis for these distinctions, there is also some external evidence from the way these diphthongs are treated in the Gothic corpus. For example, the spelling and usage in Gothic texts, particularly in the Bible translations, show a consistent pattern that suggests these were perceived as distinct sounds by the scribes. Additionally, the behavior of these diphthongs in inflectional paradigms and their interaction with other phonological processes in Gothic can provide further evidence for their distinctness. Nonetheless, the lack of detailed phonetic descriptions from the time means that the exact pronunciation and the degree of phonemic distinction between these diphthongs remains a matter of scholarly debate. texts.full_text: 'struggling to hire' since the Brexit vote Leon co-founder James Vincent Leon The co-founder of an upmarket fast food chain says it has struggled to hire European staff in the wake of Brexit. John Vincent, co-founder and chief executive of Leon, told The Telegraph that the firm's "ability to recruit has been weakened" since Brexit. Leon is an upmarket UK chain which currently has 36 restaurants in the UK, and is a widely popular lunch destination for those seeking its brand of "naturally fast food." The chain has a Mediterranean-inspired menu, and Vincent said that he wanted to "employ people who can project and understand that — people from Italy, the south of France, and so on." He said that his workforce had been "very upset" by the referendum result, as it made them feel unwelcome as immigrants, and made them feel worried about being allowed to stay. In addition, the amino acid sequence of GP25 peptide fragments will be used to construct oligonucleotide probes. These probes will be used to screen the Vero cell library for GP25 cDNA clones. texts.full_text: in the development of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Imbalanced gut microbiota can lead to an excessive pro-inflammatory environment that exacerbates intestinal inflammation by promoting pathogen colonization or impairing epithelial barrier function, which in turn facilitates the translocation of bacterial products into host tissues leading to systemic immune activation (1). The interplay between SCFAs and mucosal immunity is complex. The presence of high levels of certain bacteria such as Bacteroides fragilis can induce a local inflammatory response that leads to an increase in gut permeability, thereby enhancing the availability for fermentation by other microbes present within host environments—an effect known as priming (2). This process contributes not only towards tissue damage but also enhances microbial translocation and systemic immune activation. Furthermore, SCFAs can directly influence T cell differentiation: BT has been shown to promote Th17 responses in vitro while suppressing the development of Foxp3+ regulatory T cells—a crucial balance that maintains gut homeostasis (3). Therefore, any perturbation affecting this delicate equilibrium could potentially drive IBD pathogenesis. Dietary interventions aimed at altering microbiota composition or supplementing with specific nutrients can alter SCFA levels and influence mucosal immunity outcomes in both health maintenance as well prevention of disease development within inflammatory contexts such as those seen during active phases associated symptoms including diarrhea, abdominal pain & weight loss

texts.full_text: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) is regardedas a microvascular complication of diabetes and represents the leading cause of cardiovascular disease and end-stage chronic kidney disease ([@B16]). The incidence and prevalence of DN is growing significantly, and approximately 40% patients with diabetes have developed DN and 9% of the adult population is affected globally ([@B22]; [@B1]) and it has become one of the major threats to publichealth worldwide. Currently, the treatments of DN include intensive management of lipid,glycemic and blood pressure control as well as renin--angiotensin--aldosterone system blockade([@B18]).Nevertheless, none of these treatments are ideal for the treatment of DN and there is an urgent need to explore alternative therapeutic strategy. Inflammation is considered a critical initiator in the pathophysiology of DN and several lines of evidence have shown that some cytokines and chemokines, such as nuclear fac factor-kappa B (NF-\#x201kB) ligands play pivotal roles ([@B3]; [@B4]). These molecules are known to promote inflammation in the kidneys by activating immune cells like macrophages and endothelial cells. The activation of these pathways can lead not only to renal damage but also contribute significantly towardsvascular complications associated with diabetes, such as atherosclerosis (see Figure 1). texts.full_text: nificant roles in moderating the cognitive and functional decline among individuals with Alzheimer's disease. These factors can act as protective or compensatory mechanisms against the progression of dementia symptoms. In the study, the researchers utilized a multilevel analysis for repeated measures to evaluate how educational attainment and occupational status impact the rates of decline in cognition (measured using the Mini-Mental State Examination, or MMSE) and function (assessed with the Cleveland Scale for Activities of Daily Living). The results revealed that higher levels of education were associated with slower cognitive decline. This effect was evident even when controlling for occupational status, suggesting a direct impact of educational attainment on cognitive resilience in Alzheimer's disease. On the other hand, occupational status showed an indirect effect on cognitive decline, where individuals engaged in more cognitively demanding occupations tended to experience slower cognitive decline compared to those with less demanding jobs. This finding implies that occupational engagement may influence cognitive decline indirectly through its impact on lifestyle factors such as social interaction, physical activity, and mental stimulation. Functional decline also exhibited a moderating effect of educational attainment and occupational status. Individuals with higher education levels and more cognitively demanding occupations demonstrated slower functional decline, indicating that these factors contribute to better adaptive strategies in managing daily activities despite the cognitive challenges posed by Alzheimer's disease. texts.full_text: [3H]GBR 12935 has a rapid binding kinetics with the bovine pineal gland dopamine transporter. The disassociation phase of the binding was monitored over a period of 60 minutes, showing a Kd value of approximately 2.7 nM. This result indicates high affinity for the dopamine transporter in the bovine pineal gland. Pretreatment of the tissue with nomifensine, an inhibitor of the dopamine transporter, reduced the specific binding of [3H]GBR 12935 by approximately 85%, providing further validation that this radiological ligand specifically binds to the dopamine transporter in the pineal gland. To investigate the localization of the dopamine transporter within the pineal gland, immunofluorescent labeling techniques were employed. These methods revealed that the dopamine transporter is predominantly expressed in glial cells and some neurons of the bovine pineal gland. This study confirms the presence and characteristics of the dopamine transporter in the bovine pineal gland, which is likely to be critical for the modulation of several physiological processes within this unique organ. texts.full_text: at T = 0 K and for a range of molar volumes from 7.88 cm3/mol to 20.78 cm3/mol, from the bulk modulus and the three pure shear constants C0, C66, and C44. Three-body interactions are accounted for using our recently reported perturbative treatment based on the nonadditive three-body potential of Cencek et al. Previous studies have attempted to account for the effect of three-body interactions on the elastic properties of solid 4He; however, these calculations have treated zero point motions using either the Einstein or Debye approximations, which are insufficient in the molar volume rangeconsidered in the present work. Our VPI Monte Carlo method, on the other hand, provides a more accurate treatment of the zero point motions, which are crucial in the low-temperature regime of solid 4He. The inclusion of the nonadditive three-body potential in the VPI Monte Carlo simulations leads to a significant improvement in the agreement between the calculated elastic constants and the experimental data. Specifically, the three-body interactions are found to stiffen the solid, increasing the values of the elastic constants, particularly the shear constants, C44 and C66, and the mixed shear constant, C0. The stiffening effect is most pronounced at the lower molar volumes, where the density of the solid is higher and the interactions between the atoms are stronger. At the higher molar volumes, the effect of the three-body interactions is less pronounced, and the elastic constants are less stiff, approaching the values predicted by the pairwise interactions alone. texts.full_text: constant communication and interaction so that you can fully enjoy SHRM15 !! Pick Sessions that STRETCH you !! There are so many great sessions at this year’s Conference. Don’t fall into the trap of going to ones that reflect your day-to-day role. Stretch yourself and feed your inner desire to see what HR could look like and may look like in the future. Get on the edge of what we do and claw out of the trenches for a week. These sessions will challenge the way you think and you’ll love it. Make connections that matter !! Extroverts have to talk. They can’t help but express themselves. Use that fact to meet the folks who are at the event. We are all in HR together and instead of trying to continue to go it alone, meet the folks that are milling around you. We’ve lost sight that we’re all in the same profession. The best resource you are looking for could be right next to you. So, when you talk to strangers, because you will, connect with them so that you can be resources for each other long after the conference concludes. To put this in HR math – if you connect with 10 people and each of those folks are connected to 10 people, you have just built an incredible network !! Tweet, SnapChat, Post, Blog, etc. !! Face it, you’re already social. It’s part of your inherent nature. Social Media use by HR pros jumps through the roof during SHRM15 and that’s awesome. The challenge is that you carry it forward and remain social. Remember, social media is just another way to communicate and connect. texts.full_text: d it's password ? Something like: if user_valid(username, password): do something; I don't want to use the built-in Django authentication views or templates. I just need a simple way to verify if the provided credentials are valid. You can create a custom function that uses the `authenticate` method from `django.contrib.auth` module: ```python from django.contrib.auth import authenticate, get_user_model def user_valid(username, password): User = get_user_model() try: user = User.objects.get(username=username) except User.DoesNotExist: return False if not user.is_active: return False if user.check_password(password): return True else: return False ``` This function first retrieves the `User` model and tries to get a user instance with the provided username. If no such user exists, it returns `False`. Then it checks if the retrieved user is active (i.e., not banned or deleted). Finally, it uses the `check_password` method of the user object to verify if the password matches. You can use this function in your client-side code like so: ```python if user_valid(username='john_doe', password='my_secret'): print("User is valid") else: print("Invalid username or password") ``` Keep in mind that storing passwords as plain text (i.e., not hashed) is a significant security risk. Make sure you're using Django's built-in authentication system, which hashes and stores passwords securely. If your client needs to interact with the server via an API, consider implementing token-based authentication instead of passing username/password combos around. Loo

A: header file not found There are two ways to add external header paths: 1) Click on your target (the blue box in Project Settings) 2- Select the Build tab 3- Change Configuration from "All Configurations" to Release or Debug 4- Select Header Search Paths and type -I/path/to/header/relative/from/project/root into it. Or you can add a new path entry by clicking +. This method is required if your headers are in subdirectories. By this timeframe (March), we've seen significant strides towards cleaner energy sources with over 4GW of installed or underway capacity for coastal-based turbines across various sites around Great Britain's shores. The trajectory shows no signs of slowing down; rather than a steady incline, it seems to be more like an accelerating curve as the nation and other regions in Europe commit themselves towards their renewable energy targets. The technology has improved dramatically over time—both technically for larger turbines that can generate even greater amounts from every site location. The offshore wind farms are not only creating cleaner electricity but also providing a solid foundation to address climate change head-on, supporting thousands of jobs both directly and indirectly through the supply chain while driving innovation in manufacturing sectors as well.
